actual.topTitle: JANARDHAN REDDY V/S STATE
actual.answerSummary: This record (Janardhan Reddy v. State, 1950 LawSuit(SC) 47) concerns three criminal petitions for special leave to appeal under Article 136. The accused were charged as members of the Communist Party allegedly intent on overthrowing the Hyderabad government by violence, accused of extorting subscriptions and of abducting and murdering villagers on 21‑9‑1948. They were tried by a special tribunal established under regulations of the Military Governor and were convicted and sentenced to death in August 1949. Their appeals to the Hyderabad High Court were dismissed in December 1949, and the petitioners sought a certificate to appeal to the Judicial Committee of Hyderabad on 21‑1‑1950. The Nizam issued a firman accepting the proposed Constitution for Hyderabad on 23‑11‑1949, and the Indian Constitution became applicable to Hyderabad on 26‑1‑1950. The supplied evidence is partial and does not include the Supreme Court’s final disposition or full reasoning.

On Article 31-C, Kesavananda Bharati upheld only the first part, giving limited immunity from Articles 14 and 19 to laws implementing Article 39(b) and (c), but struck down the part that excluded judicial review of whether a law truly served those principles. Minerva Mills, on the other hand, is presented as the case that struck down Section 4 of the Forty-Second Amendment because it expanded Article 31-C far beyond what survived Kesavananda Bharati. According to the evidence, Minerva Mills invalidated the attempt to extend Article 31-C’s protection from laws implementing Article 39(b) and (c) to laws implementing any Directive Principle, and it proceeded on the basis that Article 31-C continued only in the limited form left standing by Kesavananda Bharati. So the main difference is that Kesavananda laid down the controlling limit, while Minerva Mills enforced that limit against a later constitutional amendment.
